code_blocks.code:
dataset = xdr_data
| filter _time > to_timestamp(current_time() - 86400 * 30, "SECONDS")
| filter event_type = ENUM.PROCESS and event_sub_type = ENUM.PROCESS_START
| filter lowercase(action_process_image_name) in ("powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe")
| filter lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "add-mppreference"
| filter lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "exclusionpath"
    or lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "exclusionprocess"
    or lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "exclusionextension"
| fields _time, agent_hostname, actor_effective_username,
    causality_actor_process_image_name, action_process_image_command_line
| limit 200

scenario: Microsoft Defender exclusion added at the command line.
snippet:
Scenario: imagine an operator briefly disabling protection by adding a
process, path, or extension exclusion to Microsoft Defender immediately
before deploying their tooling. The `Add-MpPreference` cmdlet is the usual
vehicle.

- https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1562/001/
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/defender/add-mppreference

Logic: legitimate exclusions are usually pushed via Group Policy or Intune, not typed at a shell. Each command-line addition is worth confirming with the host's owner and your endpoint team.
